source.title: Theta rhythms coordinate hippocampal-prefrontal interactions in a spatial memory task

confidence_rationale: Jones and Wilson 2005 established the foundational result that theta rhythms in the 4-12 Hz band coordinate hippocampal-prefrontal interactions specifically during working-memory-demanding spatial navigation, but not during simple locomotion or non-memory behavior. Both CA1 and mPFC neurons showed enhanced theta phase-locking during working-memory-requiring trials, and CA1-mPFC spike correlations were selectively elevated during the same periods. This directly supports the ThetaBuffer coupling mechanism in MECH-116: the hippocampal-to-E1 theta channel (ARC-032) is functionally engaged precisely when goal-context maintenance is required. Confidence is 0.76: the task is spatial (T-maze alternation), the mPFC role is ambiguous (decision-making vs. goal maintenance), and causality is not established.
failure_signatures: If increased hippocampal-mPFC theta coherence during spatial memory trials reflects task difficulty or arousal rather than specifically goal-context maintenance, the mapping to MECH-116's working memory substrate would be indirect
If the mPFC neurons being entrained are motor-planning neurons rather than memory-maintenance neurons, the analogy to E1 goal-context storage would be weakened
If theta coupling is only present during the decision point (T-junction) and not during the delay or approach phase, it would suggest decision integration rather than sustained goal maintenance

mapping.source_claim_statement: In a T-maze spatial alternation task requiring working memory, hippocampal CA1 neurons and mPFC neurons both showed selective theta phase-locking during memory-demanding trials; CA1-mPFC spike correlations were enhanced at theta frequency during working memory use; non-working-memory maze running did not produce the same enhancement.
mapping.ree_translation: The selective engagement of theta-band coupling between hippocampus and mPFC during working-memory-requiring behavior is the biological correlate of the ThetaBuffer channel (ARC-032) that MECH-116 relies upon to convey goal context from the hippocampal module to E1. Jones and Wilson show that this coupling is not tonic but dynamically engaged when goal-directed memory maintenance is behaviourally required -- consistent with MECH-116's claim that E1 maintains goal context 'without requiring ongoing benefit signals' but via recurrent hippocampal-frontal coupling during the delay.
mapping.mapping_caveat: T-maze alternation is a short-horizon spatial task (seconds); MECH-116 targets goal-context maintenance over multi-step harm-and-benefit trajectories. The content of what is being maintained (spatial location vs. goal/intention) may differ. The study records simultaneously from CA1 and mPFC but does not isolate the direction of coupling (feed-forward from hippocampus to mPFC vs. recurrent). The spatial task also confounds locomotion-related theta with memory-related theta.
mapping.source_context: Rat electrophysiology; T-maze spatial alternation requiring working memory vs. control linear track running (no memory required); simultaneous CA1 and mPFC tetrode recordings; theta coherence selectively elevated during T-maze but not linear track; spike correlations enhanced at theta frequency (4-12 Hz) during memory task.

confidence_components.notes: Landmark paper establishing theta coordination of hippocampal-prefrontal interactions; published in PLoS Biology with open access, highly cited. Mapping fidelity is good but reduced by spatial-task specificity and inability to isolate goal-content representation from spatial planning. Transfer risk moderate: rodent spatial task, and the LSTM is a feedforward approximation to the theta-driven recurrent coupling described.
